name: Chesterfield County Public Schools
districtFact: Chesterfield County is the fastest-growing large county in Virginia, and CCPS has absorbed that growth without the boundary chaos that typically follows rapid suburban expansion — enrollment has climbed past 64,000 while maintaining stable school assignments across its 65 schools.

about.title: About Chesterfield County Public Schools
about.content: Chesterfield County Public Schools (CCPS) serves approximately 64,000 students across 65 schools — the largest school district in the Richmond metropolitan area, and one of the fastest-growing in Virginia. The district was founded in 1869 and spans a large suburban county wrapping around the south and west of Richmond, Virginia's state capital. Its communities range from dense, established suburbs in Midlothian and Bon Air to newer growth corridors near Chester and Matoaca, and more rural areas around Ettrick and southern Chesterfield. The district includes roughly 37 elementary schools, 14 middle schools, and 9 high schools, plus alternative and special programs. Major employers in the county — HCA Virginia, VCU Health, Bon Secours, Amazon, and a growing logistics corridor along I-95 — bring a consistent stream of families relocating mid-year, making CCPS one of the more active districts in Virginia for non-August enrollment. Chesterfield County is legally separate from Richmond City and Henrico County; families must verify their school division by address before assuming which district serves them.

about.title: Academic Programs and School Choice
about.content: CCPS high schools offer Advanced Placement (AP) coursework across most academic departments, and the district participates in the regional Governor's School program for academically advanced students. Career and Technical Education (CTE) programs operate at every comprehensive high school, covering healthcare, IT, engineering, business, and the arts — aligned to the Chesterfield county economy. CCPS also operates Specialty Centers embedded within regular schools: these programs offer focused instruction in areas such as STEM, fine and performing arts, and international studies, and are available to eligible students district-wide rather than requiring a separate school transfer. Students in Chesterfield's Specialty Centers continue attending their base school for non-specialty subjects, which differs from the magnet model used in districts like Richmond City. For the most current list of Specialty Center offerings and eligibility, check the CCPS website — program availability varies by school level and can change year to year.

about.title: Enrollment and Moving to Chesterfield County
about.content: Virginia's kindergarten cutoff date is September 30: a child must turn 5 on or before September 30 to enroll in kindergarten for that school year. Kindergarten registration in Chesterfield typically opens in late winter or early spring — families should register by February or March to ensure placement before the August staggered start. Use the CCPS School Finder at oneccps.org to identify your assigned school by street address; Chesterfield, Henrico, and Richmond City have no shared boundaries, so an address that appears to be in one jurisdiction may actually fall in another. For mid-year enrollment, CCPS requires proof of residence (lease, utility bill, or mortgage statement), immunization records, birth certificate, prior school records or transcripts, and — if applicable — IEP or 504 plan documentation. The district can typically place students within a few business days of a complete application. Families relocating for hospital or healthcare employment should contact CCPS enrollment before their start date to minimize gaps in schooling.

content: Staggered first day: elementary students and 9th graders start Aug 24; 7th–8th graders and 10th–12th graders start Aug 25. Plan childcare and transportation separately for different grades in your household.
Good Friday (Mar 26) is a school holiday in CCPS, immediately before Spring Break. Combined with Spring Break (Mar 29–Apr 2), families have a 10-day window — plan travel accordingly.
CCPS uses a four-quarter system with early-release days at quarter ends. Get the full list of three-hour early dismissal days from the school office at the start of the year.
Chesterfield County is separate from Richmond City and Henrico County school divisions. Verify your school division using your specific street address at ccpsnet.net.

compareIntro: Chesterfield County Public Schools is the Richmond metro's largest district — bigger than Henrico County and far larger than Richmond City — but it's a suburban district rather than an urban one. Families comparing these three are usually asking: do I want the established suburban infrastructure of Chesterfield, the more urban-adjacent programs of Henrico, or the city magnet schools of Richmond? For families relocating from Northern Virginia, Chesterfield is often the affordability answer: suburban schools at a substantially lower cost of living than Fairfax or Loudoun.

districtFaqs.q: When does the school year typically start for Chesterfield County Public Schools?
districtFaqs.a: Chesterfield County Public Schools typically begins the school year in the third or fourth week of August. The district uses a staggered first-day schedule: elementary students (grades 1–5), rising 6th graders, and rising 9th graders begin one day before students in grades 7–8 and 10–12. The exact first-day dates vary by year — check the current CCPS academic calendar at oneccps.org for the specific start dates each school year.

districtFaqs.q: Is Chesterfield County Public Schools the same as Richmond City Public Schools?
districtFaqs.a: No. Chesterfield County Public Schools and Richmond City Public Schools are separate, independent school divisions serving different jurisdictions in the Richmond metropolitan area. They have different school boards, calendars, and school boundaries. Families new to the Richmond area should confirm which school division serves their specific address at each division's website.

districtFaqs.q: What is the four-quarter system in Chesterfield County?
districtFaqs.a: Chesterfield County Public Schools organizes the academic year into four quarters rather than two traditional semesters. Quarter one typically runs from the first day of school through late October; quarter two runs through mid-to-late January; quarter three ends around late March; and quarter four ends on the last day of school in late May or early June. Each quarter end is associated with three-hour early-release days and parent conference periods. Report cards are issued four times per year rather than twice. Families coming from semester-based districts should check the CCPS academic calendar each year for the specific quarter-end and early-release dates.

districtFaqs.q: Why does CCPS stagger the first day of school?
districtFaqs.a: Chesterfield County Public Schools staggers the first day of school to give staff more time to support students transitioning to a new school level. Elementary students (grades 1–5), 6th graders entering middle school, and 9th graders entering high school start one day before students in grades 7–8 and 10–12. On the first day, staff can focus on orienting new-to-level students before the full school population arrives. Households with children across multiple grade levels need separate childcare and transportation plans for the two different first days — check the current CCPS calendar for the specific dates each year.

districtFaqs.q: Does CCPS follow the same calendar as Henrico County Public Schools?
districtFaqs.a: No. Chesterfield County Public Schools and Henrico County Public Schools are separate, independent school divisions with different academic calendars. While both typically start and end within a few days of each other, specific dates for teacher workdays, early-release days, and spring break may differ. Families with children in both divisions — or coordinating with grandparents or childcare providers in Henrico — should verify each division's calendar separately each year.

districtFaqs.q: Is Good Friday always a school holiday in Chesterfield County?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. Good Friday is observed as a school holiday in Chesterfield County Public Schools and is a longstanding practice across Virginia's public school divisions. Good Friday falls in late March or early April depending on the year — its relationship to Spring Break varies: in some years it falls immediately before Spring Break, creating a longer combined break window; in other years it falls one or two weeks after Spring Break ends as a standalone holiday. Families relocating from states where Good Friday is not a school holiday may not expect this day off — check the official CCPS calendar each year for the exact date.

districtFaqs.q: What are three-hour early release days in CCPS?
districtFaqs.a: Three-hour early release days (also called three-hour early dismissal days) are scheduled throughout the school year in Chesterfield County for teacher professional development and parent conferences. On these days, students are released approximately three hours earlier than the normal dismissal time. These days typically cluster around the end of each academic quarter — roughly late October, mid-to-late January, and late March — but the exact dates change each year. Get the full list of early release dates from the CCPS academic calendar or your school's office at the start of each school year, as the specific schedule can vary by school.

districtFaqs.q: How do I find my assigned school in Chesterfield County?
districtFaqs.a: Use the School Finder tool at oneccps.org to look up your assigned elementary, middle, and high school by street address. Chesterfield County, Henrico County, and Richmond City are separate school divisions with no shared attendance zones — an address in a Chesterfield zip code does not automatically mean your child attends a CCPS school, and vice versa. Always enter your specific street address rather than relying on zip code alone. If you are moving and don't have a final address yet, the CCPS enrollment office can advise on school assignments based on the neighborhoods or subdivisions you are considering.

districtFaqs.q: Is my child eligible for a school bus in Chesterfield County?
districtFaqs.a: Chesterfield County Public Schools provides bus transportation to students who live beyond the walking distance threshold for their assigned school. Generally, elementary students living more than 1.5 miles from school and middle/high school students living more than 2 miles are eligible, though the exact thresholds can vary by school and are subject to CCPS Transportation Department policy. Students living within walking distance are not typically assigned a bus stop. After enrollment, confirm your child's bus route and stop location with the school or CCPS Transportation. Route information is usually finalized and distributed in the week before school starts.

districtFaqs.q: When should I register my child for kindergarten in Chesterfield County?
districtFaqs.a: Virginia's kindergarten cutoff is September 30 — your child must turn 5 on or before September 30 to enroll in kindergarten for that school year. Chesterfield County Public Schools typically opens kindergarten registration in late winter or early spring, usually February or March. Registering early is recommended for families who want placement at a specific school or Specialty Center program before spots fill. Required documents include the child's birth certificate, up-to-date Virginia immunization records, and proof of Chesterfield County residence. Pre-K (for income-eligible or developmentally qualified students) has a separate registration process — contact CCPS directly for current Pre-K availability.

districtFaqs.q: How does CCPS handle snow days and weather closures?
districtFaqs.a: Chesterfield County Public Schools announces school closings and delays through its website, the CCPS app, and local TV stations (particularly WTVR CBS 6 and WRIC ABC 8). CCPS typically uses a two-hour delay option for borderline weather — school runs two hours later than normal, allowing roads to be treated before buses run. Full closures are called when conditions are too severe for safe transportation. Virginia school divisions are required to build makeup days into the calendar or convert closure days to remote learning days; CCPS typically schedules a small number of built-in makeup days near the end of the year. Check the CCPS calendar for any designated makeup day dates and note that the school year end date may shift if multiple closure days are used.

districtFaqs.q: What documents do I need to enroll in Chesterfield County Public Schools?
districtFaqs.a: To enroll a student in CCPS, you will need: proof of Chesterfield County residency (lease agreement, mortgage statement, or current utility bill in your name), the student's birth certificate, Virginia-compliant immunization records (or a medical or religious exemption form), and prior school records or a transcript from the previous school. For students transferring mid-year, a current report card or grade transcript is also helpful to assist with course placement. If the student has an IEP, 504 plan, or receives special education services, bring a copy — CCPS is required to honor existing accommodations promptly. Pre-K enrollment has separate requirements; contact CCPS directly for current eligibility criteria.

livingHere.intro: Chesterfield County wraps around the south and west of Richmond, Virginia's state capital, offering suburban living with easy access to Richmond's cultural institutions, the James River, and I-95 — at significantly lower cost than Northern Virginia. The county is undergoing an economic transition away from its tobacco-industry past toward healthcare, logistics, and financial services.

highlights.label: Richmond International Airport (RIC)
highlights.detail: RIC is 20–30 minutes from most Chesterfield neighborhoods and is the departure point for most CCPS family travel. The school calendar defines the three busiest booking windows: Thanksgiving week (CCPS observes the full week), the winter break period around Christmas, and the stretch from CCPS's June 4 last day through early July. Book holiday flights as soon as the CCPS calendar is released each spring — RIC has fewer nonstop options than Dulles or BWI, so fares and award seats on peak-season dates fill faster.

highlights.label: James River — Summer and End-of-Year
highlights.detail: The James River is the dominant end-of-school and summer destination for Chesterfield families. Youth paddling programs and river camps near Pony Pasture Rapids and Huguenot Flatwater fill in March and April — register as soon as spring break begins on March 29 to secure summer spots. Kayak and canoe rentals along the James book out quickly the weekend after CCPS's June 4 last day. The river also features in the CCPS science and outdoor education curriculum, particularly in middle school environmental studies units.

highlights.label: Civil War Heritage and CCPS Field Trips
highlights.detail: Richmond served as the Confederate capital, and Chesterfield borders major battlefields including Cold Harbor and the Petersburg National Battlefield. These sites are central to CCPS's Virginia Studies curriculum: 4th and 5th graders regularly take curriculum-tied field trips to the battlefields and Richmond's Civil War landmarks as part of Virginia history requirements. Families new to the district should expect at least one battlefield or Richmond history field trip per year in elementary grades — permission slips and chaperone signups typically go out 2–3 weeks in advance.

highlights.label: Healthcare Sector and Mid-Year Enrollment
highlights.detail: HCA Virginia, VCU Health, and Bon Secours Richmond operate major facilities in or adjacent to Chesterfield, drawing a steady stream of medical professionals and hospital staff to the county. Many healthcare employees arrive on rotating schedules or mid-year transfer assignments — CCPS regularly processes enrollment outside the traditional August window. Families relocating for hospital positions should contact CCPS enrollment before their start date; the district can typically place students within days of a completed application.

highlights.label: Staggered First Day of School
highlights.detail: CCPS uses a phased school opening — elementary, 6th, and 9th graders start August 24; other grades start August 25 — so households with children at multiple grade levels need separate first-day childcare and transportation plans.

highlights.label: Kings Dominion — Season and School Calendar
highlights.detail: Kings Dominion, Virginia's largest amusement park, is 25 miles north of Midlothian in Doswell and a regular CCPS family destination. Season passes bought in winter pay off quickly: Opening Weekend (typically late March or early April) falls just before CCPS's spring break window (March 29–April 2), giving pass holders their lowest-crowd visits of the season. The park runs through October, overlapping with the first quarter of school — weekend visits are common through Q1 before homework and activity schedules increase. CCPS school groups occasionally book end-of-year trips in late May.

highlights.label: Midlothian — Where Families Relocate First
highlights.detail: Midlothian is where most families new to Chesterfield County choose to live — it's the densest suburban corridor, with established neighborhoods served by multiple well-regarded elementary and high schools. Fall registration in Midlothian fills quickly: popular elementary schools in the Hull Street Road and Coalfield Road corridors reach capacity in early August. Families relocating from out of state should contact CCPS enrollment by July to confirm school assignment and supply lists ahead of the August 24 staggered first day. New residents should also allow extra time to learn school-day traffic patterns on Midlothian Turnpike, which backs up significantly during morning drop-off.

highlights.label: Quarter-End Rhythms for CCPS Families
highlights.detail: Chesterfield's four-quarter calendar creates a predictable annual rhythm that shapes family scheduling. Parent-teacher conferences and three-hour early release days cluster around each quarter end — October 30, January 22, and March 25 — so arranging afternoon childcare on those dates is a recurring need. Report cards are issued at each quarter's close, giving families four check-ins per year rather than two. Families new to a quarter-based system (especially those coming from semester-based districts in other states) should download the CCPS academic calendar on the first day of school and mark all early-release and conference dates immediately.

relatedDistricts.name: Fairfax County Public Schools
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Fairfax County is Virginia's largest and most academically competitive district, with extensive magnet, IB, and Governor's School options. Chesterfield is the affordability alternative for families priced out of Northern Virginia — similar suburban suburban infrastructure at significantly lower housing costs, but with fewer specialized program options at the secondary level.

relatedDistricts.name: Virginia Beach City Public Schools
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Virginia Beach City Public Schools serves the Hampton Roads metro and has a significant military family population (Naval Station Norfolk is nearby). Chesterfield has no comparable military presence, but both are large suburban Virginia districts with traditional calendar structures and similar quarter-based academic years.

relatedDistricts.name: Prince William County Public Schools
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Prince William County is in Northern Virginia and shares some calendar structure similarities with Chesterfield, but serves a different economic and demographic profile — more commuter-focused, closer to DC. Families comparing these two are typically choosing between Northern Virginia access and Richmond-area affordability.
